message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

README.md has an extended complaint about google 'eventual consistency'.  I think we ought to make a similar though perhaps shorter complaint section about quay's practice of churning podman vm images rapidly- a key justification for project Lodes is to anchor safely the podman vm images to avoid getting broken asynchronously, which definitely happened to me.  Orient yourself- do we have such a section yet?  Check RBS0*.adoc too for a statement/ treatment of this.

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Add quay image consistency complaint to docs

content.text: I'll orient myself. Let me find the existing "eventual consistency" complaint in the README and check what (if anything) RBS0 specs say about image churn / Lodes anchoring.

input.description: Find eventual consistency mention in README

aiTitle: Add quay image consistency complaint to docs

content.text: Let me read the existing complaint and the README's structure, plus the relevant RBS0 Lode/anchor passages.

content.content: 500 Base images are vetted separately: digest-pinned at [Enshrine](#Enshrine) time, inspectable via [SBOM](#SBOM), and stored as project-owned copies in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 501 A [Tethered](#Tethered) build of the base image followed by an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build of your application is the expected pattern — the base image is a known input, your proprietary layers are the protected output. 502 503 **Regulatory alignment.** 504 No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 505 506 ## Appendix: Eventual Consistency and the Missing Completion Contract 507 508 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is built on cloud APIs, and cloud APIs are *eventually consistent*: when you mutate state — grant a role, delete a [service account](#Governor), link billing — the change does not take effect everywhere at once. 509 It propagates across replicas over seconds, occasionally minutes. 510 For systems that are read on essentially every API call across the globe, choosing fast, always-available reads over instantly-consistent ones is a defensible engineering tradeoff, and we grant it without complaint. 511 512 The defensible part is the consistency *model*. 513 The indefensible part is what the term quietly omits: a **completion contract**. 514 When a mutating call returns, it tells you nothing about whether the work is finished — there is no terminal-state signal you can poll to learn that the change has settled. 515 This is not a law of physics. 516 The pattern for providing it is well understood and widely shipped: Google's own API design guidelines define long-running operations with a `done` flag and a terminal state, and Azure's Resource Manager specifies an async-operation contract end to end. 517 The giants deliver it excellently in places — and then withhold it on exactly the operations that race: IAM propagation, billing linkage, identity lifecycle. 518 The capability exists; it is selectively absent where it would matter most. 519 520 Without a completion contract, every consumer independently reinvents the same retry-poll-tolerate scaffolding to compensate. 521 The honest version of that scaffolding polls for an *observable terminal state*; the dishonest version, which the gap quietly encourages, is a blind `sleep N` — a guessed magic number standing in for a signal that should have existed. 522 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) holds the honest line where it can: it polls for the real state, requires *consecutive* confirming reads before believing a transition (debouncing the flap rather than trusting the first answer), and bounds every wait with a timeout so a never-settling operation fails loudly instead of hanging. 523 524 The sharpest instance is service-account deletion. 525 The delete returns an empty success with no operation handle, and the account is not actually removed — it is *soft-deleted*, recoverable for thirty days. 526 So even the simplest question, "is it gone; can I reuse the name?", has no clean answer: a read of the just-deleted account flaps between "present" and "absent" across replicas while the tombstone propagates, and no API will tell you when the name is safe to reuse. 527 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Divest](#Divest) path copes by treating the account as durably gone only after several consecutive "not found" reads — see the ["already exists" after a delete](#StaleDeleteRead) diagnostic for the symptom this produces. 528 529 Plainly: this is not a hard distributed-systems problem. 530 It is a completion contract the vendors chose not to provide, dressed in distributed-systems vocabulary. 531 "Eventual consistency" accurately describes the read path; here it is also a polite way of saying *we will not tell you when we are finished*. 532 We depend on Google Cloud and expect to keep depending on it — and building atop eventually-consistent APIs with no completion contract is still crappy engineering on the vendor's part, on exactly the surfaces where getting it right would cost them nothing in their consistency model. 533 534 ## Appendix: Roadmap 535 536 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 537 538 - **[Crucible Conduit for Cloud Services](#CrucibleConduit)** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 539 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 540 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 541 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 542 543 - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. 544 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. 545 Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). 546 547 - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - The path to corporate-acceptable identity — admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of long-lived service-account keys on disk, with identity proved by a fresh sign-in and capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret at rest. 548 This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. 549 Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than a manual, per-key [Divest](#Divest). 550 Critical sequences, rendered from committed PlantUML source through the [pluml](#pluml) [Crucible](#Crucible): 551 [Federation login](diagrams/federation-login.svg) (two-leg device-flow + STS exchange) · 552 [Federation setup](diagrams/federation-setup.svg) (Payor-side workforce pool + provider) · 553 [Keyfile login](diagrams/keyfile-login.svg) (today's tier, for contrast) · 554 [The single code seam](diagrams/federation-seam.svg) (one accessor, mode-enum branch — the structural payoff). 555 556 - **[VPC Service Controls](#VpcServiceControls)** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 557 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 558 If a VPC is stood up for the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit) architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 559 560 - **[Cosign Container Signing](#CosignSigning)** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 561 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 562 563 - **[CDN-Aware IP Gating](#CdnAwareIpGating)** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. 564 A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 565 566 - **[Podman Support](#PodmanSupport)** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. 567 On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 568 Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 569 570 - **[Crucible-to-Crucible Networking](#CrucibleToCrucible)** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). 571 The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 572 573 ## Appendix: Reference Project 574 575 This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). 576 The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. 577 578 | Path | Description | 579 |------|-------------| 580 | `Project Root/` | | 581 | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | 582 | `├── RELEASE.md` | [Release Procedure](#ReleaseProcedure) — maintainer release qualification ceremony | 583 | `├── tt/` | [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | 584 | `├── Tools/` | | 585 | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | 586 | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | 587 | `└── rbmm_moorings/` | Consumer config root — [BURC](#BURC) + Recipe Bottle [Regimes](#Regime) + [Vessels](#Vessel) | 588 | ` ├── burc.env` | [BURC](#BURC) — project structure (tabtarget, tools, temp/output dirs) | 589 | ` ├── rbrp.env` | [RBRP](#RBRP) — [Payor](#Payor) identity for this [Depot](#Depot) | 590 | ` ├── rbrr.env` | [RBRR](#RBRR) — Repository-wide configuration shared across all operations | 591 | ` ├── rbrd.env` | [RBRD](#RBRD) — [Depot](#Depot) identity (frozen at [Levy](#Levy)) | 592 | ` ├── ccyolo/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox | 593 | ` │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Claude Code, Anthropic-only allowlist | 594 | ` ├── tadmor/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [tadmor](#tadmor) adversarial testing | 595 | ` │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 596 | ` ├── moriah/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [moriah](#moriah) airgap-built adversarial testing | 597 | ` │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 598 | ` ├── srjcl/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — Jupyter notebook | 599 | ` │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Jupyter, academic-domain allowlist | 600 | ` ├── pluml/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — PlantUML diagram server | 601 | ` │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + PlantUML, no-egress allowlist | 602 | ` ├── rbml_launchers/` | Launcher scripts (environment gates) — `launcher.*.sh` | 603 | ` └── rbmv_vessels/` | [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions | 604 | ` ├── common-sentry-context/` | Shared [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle) build context | 605 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | debian-slim + iptables + dnsmasq | 606 | ` │ ├── rbjs_sentry.sh` | [Sentry](#Sentry) runtime — policy engine | 607 | ` │ └── rbjp_pentacle.sh` | [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runtime — namespace setup | 608 | ` ├── rbev-sentry-deb-tether/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Sentry](#Sentry) (tethered, upstream pull) | 609 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 610 | ` ├── rbev-sentry-deb-airgap/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Sentry](#Sentry) (airgapped, enshrined bases) | 611 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, airgap egress | 612 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ccyolo/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox | 613 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | node:22-slim + SSH + Claude Code | 614 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 615 | ` ├── common-ifrit-context/` | Shared [Ifrit](#Ifrit) build context (tether + airgap variants) | 616 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile.tether` | Rust binary + network attack tools (tether build) | 617 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile.airgap` | Same image built `FROM` the forge ([Airgap](#Airgap) build, no upstream pull) | 618 | ` │ ├── Cargo.toml` | Ifrit crate manifest | 619 | ` │ └── src/` | Ifrit source (rbid binary) | 620 | ` ├── common-ifrit-forge-context/` | Build context for the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) forge — warms cargo cache + pre-stages apt deps | 621 | ` │ └── Dockerfile` | Forge image; consumed by `Dockerfile.airgap` as its base | 622 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) (tether) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary | 623 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 624 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) ([Airgap](#Airgap)) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary, [Ordained](#Ordain) without upstream pull | 625 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, airgap egress | 626 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) (tether) — Build-time forge fixture for [Airgap](#Airgap) [Ifrit](#Ifrit) | 627 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 628 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-plantuml/` | [Bind](#Bind) — upstream image pinned by digest | 629 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Bind](#Bind) mode, digest reference | 630 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — Jupyter notebook server | 631 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | | 632 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 633 | ` └── (4 additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | 634 635 ## Appendix: Specific Regimes 636 637 **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. 638 [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. 639 640 **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. 641 Not in git. 642 Log directory, station paths. 643 644 **[RBRR](#RBRR)** — Repository-wide configuration: runtime container prefix, vessel directory, container DNS, Cloud Build timeouts, secrets directory, public docs URL. 645 646 **[RBRD](#RBRD)** — [Depot](#Depot) identity — cloud prefix, depot moniker, GCP region, Cloud Build pool machine type — populated during [Levy](#Levy) and frozen for the depot's productive lifetime; changing any field after [Levy](#Levy) requires a fresh [Depot](#Depot). 647 648 **[RBRP](#RBRP)** — [Manor](#Manor) identity — billing account, OAuth client ID, operator email, and the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project. 649 In the repo. 650 651 **[RBRO](#RBRO)** — [Payor](#Payor) OAuth credentials — client secret and refresh token. 652 Not in the repo. 653 654 **[RBRA](#RBRA)** — Role credentials resident on user workstation, enabling [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 655 One credential file per role per [Depot](#Depot). 656 657 **[RBRV](#RBRV)** — [Vessel](#Vessel) configuration specifying [Bind](#Bind), [Conjure](#Conjure), or [Graft](#Graft) mode for creating [Hallmarks](#Hallmark). 658 659 **[RBRN](#RBRN)** — Per-[Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Crucible](#Crucible) configuration mapping two [Vessels](#Vessel) — [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) — with runtime and [Hallmark](#Hallmark) assignments. 660 661 ## License 662 663 Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. 664 665 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - The path to corporate-acceptable identity — admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of long-lived service-account keys on disk, with identity proved by a fresh sign-in and capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret at rest. This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than a manual, per-key [Divest](#Divest). Critical sequences, rendered from committed PlantUML source through the [pluml](#pluml) [Crucible](#Crucible): [Federation login](diagrams/federation-login.svg) (two-leg device-flow + STS exchange) · [Federation setup](diagrams/federation-setup.svg) (Payor-side workforce pool + provider) · [Keyfile login](diagrams/keyfile-login.svg) (today's tier, for contrast) · [The single code seam](diagrams/federation-seam.svg) (one accessor, mode-enum branch — the structural payoff). - **[VPC Service Controls](#VpcServiceControls)** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. If a VPC is stood up for the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit) architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. - **[Cosign Container Signing](#CosignSigning)** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. - **[CDN-Aware IP Gating](#CdnAwareIpGating)** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. - **[Podman Support](#PodmanSupport)** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. - **[Crucible-to-Crucible Networking](#CrucibleToCrucible)** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. ## Appendix: Reference Project This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. | Path | Description | |------|-------------| | `Project Root/` | | | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | | `├── RELEASE.md` | [Release Procedure](#ReleaseProcedure) — maintainer release qualification ceremony | | `├── tt/` | [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | | `├── Tools/` | | | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | | `└── rbmm_moorings/` | Consumer config root — [BURC](#BURC) + Recipe Bottle [Regimes](#Regime) + [Vessels](#Vessel) | | ` ├── burc.env` | [BURC](#BURC) — project structure (tabtarget, tools, rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | | ` └── (4 additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | ## Appendix: Specific Regimes **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. Not in git. Log directory, station paths. **[RBRR](#RBRR)** — Repository-wide configuration: runtime container prefix, vessel directory, container DNS, Cloud Build timeouts, secrets directory, public docs URL. **[RBRD](#RBRD)** — [Depot](#Depot) identity — cloud prefix, depot moniker, GCP region, Cloud Build pool machine type — populated during [Levy](#Levy) and frozen for the depot's productive lifetime; changing any field after [Levy](#Levy) requires a fresh [Depot](#Depot). **[RBRP](#RBRP)** — [Manor](#Manor) identity — billing account, OAuth client ID, operator email, and the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project. In the repo. **[RBRO](#RBRO)** — [Payor](#Payor) OAuth credentials — client secret and refresh token. Not in the repo. **[RBRA](#RBRA)** — Role credentials resident on user workstation, enabling [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. One credential file per role per [Depot](#Depot). **[RBRV](#RBRV)** — [Vessel](#Vessel) configuration specifying [Bind](#Bind), [Conjure](#Conjure), or [Graft](#Graft) mode for creating [Hallmarks](#Hallmark). **[RBRN](#RBRN)** — Per-[Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Crucible](#Crucible) configuration mapping two [Vessels](#Vessel) — [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) — with runtime and [Hallmark](#Hallmark) assignments. ## License Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. content.text: Now let me check whether the README mentions Lodes/Quay at all, and look at how RBS0 frames the *rationale* for Lodes (vs. just the mechanics I see above).

content.content: === README mentions of Lode/Quay/Podman VM/anchor/churn === 17:> The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 76:Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) ([egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) build from source with [SLSA provenance](#Provenance)), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 79:The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 137:[Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files to pin a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to specific image versions. 204:Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 212:The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. 252:The [pluml](#pluml) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with a [Bind](#Bind)-mode PlantUML [Bottle](#Bottle) — an upstream image pinned by digest — under a no-egress allowlist: the renderer needs no internet, so the [Crucible](#Crucible) grants it none. It exercises the [Bind](#Bind) supply-chain path and the most restrictive network posture. 265:Knowing where an image came from — and proving it — is the domain of software supply-chain security. The stabilized toolchain pairs build provenance ([SLSA](https://slsa.dev)) with cryptographic signing ([Sigstore](https://www.sigstore.dev)/cosign), a [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft)), vulnerability scanning, and deploy-time admission control ([Kyverno](https://kyverno.io), OPA Gatekeeper) that rejects unsigned or unattested images. 336:[Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation and [SBOMs](#SBOM). 339:**[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. 341:Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build [provenance](#Provenance). 366:The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no [provenance](#Provenance) chain. 461:| [**Bind**](#Bind) | Digest-pin comparison — image in GAR matches pinned upstream reference | Digest-pin match | 472:[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) generates an [SBOM](#SBOM) for every build using [Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft), scanning each per-platform image during the [About](#About) assembly step. 495:Builder tool images enter through a parallel gate: the [Reliquary](#Reliquary), inscribed once and pinned by digest for all subsequent builds. 500:Base images are vetted separately: digest-pinned at [Enshrine](#Enshrine) time, inspectable via [SBOM](#SBOM), and stored as project-owned copies in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 628:| ` ├── rbev-bottle-plantuml/` | [Bind](#Bind) — upstream image pinned by digest | 659:**[RBRN](#RBRN)** — Per-[Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Crucible](#Crucible) configuration mapping two [Vessels](#Vessel) — [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) — with runtime and [Hallmark](#Hallmark) assignments.

1342	=== {rbtgo_ark_conjure}
1343	
1344	Conjures an
1345	{rbtga_ark_image}
1346	by building a container image
1347	({rbtgog_ark})
1348	from a vessel specification.
1349	Build context delivered via {rbtga_ark_pouch}, submitted via `builds.create` API. The combined job produces both the `image` and `about` packages under the hallmark subtree (about steps embedded via stitch). Vouch is a separate Cloud Build job using DSSE envelope signature verification.

1356

1358	{gar_service}
1359	({rbtgog_ark}),
1360	producing content-addressed
1361	{rbrv_image_anchor}
1362	references from
1363	{rbrv_image_origin}
1364	declarations.
1365	The
1366	{rbtr_director}
1367	submits a Cloud Build job that copies each upstream image by tag to GAR with a human-readable anchor tag, and writes the anchor back to the vessel regime.

1376	{rbtgi_repository}
1377	({rbtgog_ark})
1378	via native docker/podman push.
1379	The image push itself is local (no Cloud Build); about and vouch follow as Cloud Build jobs.

1388	({rbtgog_ark}).
1389	No registry push, no Cloud Build, no credentials — `docker build` runs against the workstation cache and produces a `k`-prefixed
1390	{rbtga_hallmark}
1391	that composes cleanly with nameplate and compose infrastructure but is obviously not a production hallmark.
1392	Kludge resolves
1393	{rbrv_image_anchor}
1394	declarations to GAR references identically to {rbtgo_ark_conjure}, but anchored images must already be locally cached; the credentialed fetch happens out-of-band via {rbtgo_image_retrieve}. Origin-only slots auto-pull inline — no credentials required for public upstream.

/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc:219:Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.

Auditor reaction: "Interesting — justify it." That question alone disqualifies it for a trust boundary.
/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSLA-lode_augur.adoc:85:Trust posture is reported honestly per the envelope's declared grade: a `recorded-at-acquisition` Lode states that RB attests only the digest observed at capture, never that it remains re-checkable against a vanished upstream.

2150	[[rbtga_lode]]
2151	//axl_voices axig_artifact_group
2152	{rbtga_lode}::
2153	The fetched-side capture package: one
2154	{gar_service}
2155	package under the `{gar_lodes_namespace}/` namespace, named by its
2156	{rbtga_touchmark},
2157	holding an upstream artifact captured from outside the project together with its provenance.
2158	The Ark-parallel of the made side — where an
2159	{rbtga_ark}
2160	is the coherent unit of a *built* output, a Lode is the coherent unit of a *captured* one.
2161	+
2162	One Lode is exactly one GAR package, and that package IS the atomic-delete unit: a single `packages delete` removes the Lode and every member version and tag in one call (see {rbtgo_lode_banish}).
2163	Members and provenance ride as *tags within the one package*, never as `/`-path-segments — GAR has no subtree delete, so a slash would make sibling packages that cannot be removed atomically.
2164	+
2165	Single and compound Lodes are the same shape — a cardinality attribute, not a structural distinction.
2166	A bole Lode is a 1-member package; a forthcoming reliquary or podvm cohort is an N-member package.
2167	Same delete primitive, same provenance attachment point, same wire shape.
2168	+
2169	For the bole kind, the member tags on the package are:
2170	+
2171	* `:rbi_bole` — uniform greppable handle for the singleton base
2172	* `:rbi_sha256-«HEX»` — the canonical OCI digest tag (matches what every tool reports; the exact cross-Lode dedup key)
2173	* `:«SANITIZED-ORIGIN»-«SHA10»` — an unsprued glance fingerprint (origin cue + 10-hex digest prefix)
2174	* `:rbi_vouch` — the one-per-Lode {rbtga_lode_vouch} provenance envelope
2175	+
2176	The `rbi_` sprue marks strings from RB's own domain — RB's authored lexicon (`bole`, `vouch`) and RB-measured-from-content values (the digest) — and is withheld from foreign-cued strings such as the sanitized origin.
2177	+
2178	Lode acquisition runs cloud-side, never the workstation: every kind fetches its bytes over the network from within GCP, trusting the GCP environment over the operator's potentially-compromised station.
2179	A Lode is banished atomically by {rbtgo_lode_banish}.
2180	
2181	[[rbtga_touchmark]]
2182	//axl_voices axtg_timestamp
2183	{rbtga_touchmark}::
2184	The identifier marking a captured
2185	{rbtga_lode}
2186	— the fetched-side parallel of the
2187	{rbtga_hallmark}.
2188	Minted host-side before submission, so the touchmark is known before the build (the host owns the stamp in order to write the {rbf_fact_lode}).
2189	+
2190	Format: `«KIND»«YYMMDDHHMMSS»`, where `«KIND»` is the one- or two-letter kind code and the datestamp matches the hallmark's second-granular form.
2191	Kind letters: `b` bole, `t` tool, `r` reliquary, `w` wsl, `vw` podvm-wsl, `vn` podvm-native.
2192	Example: `b260602120000`.
2193	+
2194	The shared `-mark` suffix with {rbtga_hallmark} is a deliberate role-signal: any `*mark` is a specific-instance handle; the first syllable carries the side — `hall-` what we forged, `touch-` what we captured.
2195	The touchmark is the GAR path segment: the Lode package is `{gar_lodes_namespace}/«TOUCHMARK»`, so one value names the whole package.
2196	See {rbst_touchmark} for the string type and regex.
2197	
2198	[[rbtga_lode_vouch]]
2199	//axl_voices axig_verification_artifact
2200	{rbtga_lode_vouch}::
2201	The provenance envelope carried by every
2202	{rbtga_lode}
2203	— one per Lode, batch-level — recording *capture-fidelity* rather than build-integrity.
2204	The fetched-side parallel to the made-side
2205	{rbtga_ark_vouch},
2206	and a different claim: "we faithfully captured upstream X at digest D," not "we built this securely."
2207	+
2208	It has one canonical home: the in-GAR `:rbi_vouch` tag (the Lode self-describing in the registry, decoded by {rbtgo_lode_augur}). The host-side {rbf_fact_lode} carries only the bare touchmark, not the envelope — so reading host-side provenance costs a GAR call, the accepted price of a single canonical home.
2209	+
2210	Fields (schema `rbld-vouch-1`, near-term unsigned — `signature: null` — but schema-versioned):
2211	`schema`, `kind`, `lode` (the touchmark), `acquired_at` (ISO-8601 UTC), `acquired_by` (the capturing Cloud Build service account), `capture_build` (the capture's own build id), `trust_grade`, and `members[]`.
2212	Each `members[]` entry carries `name`, `origin`, `digest`, `verification`, and its assigned `tags` — so the captured identity and any Director-assigned names are themselves attested.
2213	`members[]` is the cardinality axis: length 1 for singletons (bole), N for cohorts.
2214	+
2215	Two honest trust grades, declared per Lode (Palisade honesty — never over-claim):
2216	+
2217	* `verified-against-published` — bytes re-checkable against a persistent upstream (OCI content-address, or an out-of-band published checksum). The bole kind carries this grade.
2218	* `recorded-at-acquisition` — upstream offers no durable re-checkable reference (e.g. quay rotates podvm images out within days), so RB attests only the digest observed at capture. Reserved for the podvm kinds.
2219	+
2220	Future signing is reserved via cosign or the OCI referrers API once GAR maturity is confirmed.
2221	
2222	=== Foundry Fact Files
2223	
2224	[[rbf_fact_hallmark]]
2225	//axpof_fact
2226	{rbf_fact_hallmark}::
2227	Hallmark string.
2228	
2229	[[rbf_fact_build_id]]
2230	//axpof_fact
2231	{rbf_fact_build_id}::
2232	Cloud Build job ID.
2233	
2234	[[rbf_fact_gar_root]]
2235	//axpof_fact
2236	{rbf_fact_gar_root}::
2237	GAR registry root path.
2238	
2239	[[rbf_fact_ark_stem]]

[[rbtga_lode]]
//axl_voices axig_artifact_group
{rbtga_lode}::
The fetched-side capture package: one
{gar_service}
package under the `{gar_lodes_namespace}/` namespace, named by its
{rbtga_touchmark},
holding an upstream artifact captured from outside the project together with its provenance.
The Ark-parallel of the made side — where an
{rbtga_ark}
is the coherent unit of a *built* output, a Lode is the coherent unit of a *captured* one.
+
One Lode is exactly one GAR package, and that package IS the atomic-delete unit: a single `packages delete` removes the Lode and every member version and tag in one call (see {rbtgo_lode_banish}).
Members and provenance ride as *tags within the one package*, never as `/`-path-segments — GAR has no subtree delete, so a slash would make sibling packages that cannot be removed atomically.
+
Single and compound Lodes are the same shape — a cardinality attribute, not a structural distinction.
A bole Lode is a 1-member package; a forthcoming reliquary or podvm cohort is an N-member package.
Same delete primitive, same provenance attachment point, same wire shape.
+
For the bole kind, the member tags on the package are:
+
* `:rbi_bole` — uniform greppable handle for the singleton base
* `:rbi_sha256-«HEX»` — the canonical OCI digest tag (matches what every tool reports; the exact cross-Lode dedup key)
* `:«SANITIZED-ORIGIN»-«SHA10»` — an unsprued glance fingerprint (origin cue + 10-hex digest prefix)
* `:rbi_vouch` — the one-per-Lode {rbtga_lode_vouch} provenance envelope
+
The `rbi_` sprue marks strings from RB's own domain — RB's authored lexicon (`bole`, `vouch`) and RB-measured-from-content values (the digest) — and is withheld from foreign-cued strings such as the sanitized origin.
+
Lode acquisition runs cloud-side, never the workstation: every kind fetches its bytes over the network from within GCP, trusting the GCP environment over the operator's potentially-compromised station.
A Lode is banished atomically by {rbtgo_lode_banish}.

[[rbtga_touchmark]]
//axl_voices axtg_timestamp
{rbtga_touchmark}::
The identifier marking a captured
{rbtga_lode}
— the fetched-side parallel of the
{rbtga_hallmark}.
Minted host-side before submission, so the touchmark is known before the build (the host owns the stamp in order to write the {rbf_fact_lode}).
+
Format: `«KIND»«YYMMDDHHMMSS»`, where `«KIND»` is the one- or two-letter kind code and the datestamp matches the hallmark's second-granular form.
Kind letters: `b` bole, `t` tool, `r` reliquary, `w` wsl, `vw` podvm-wsl, `vn` podvm-native.
Example: `b260602120000`.
+
The shared `-mark` suffix with {rbtga_hallmark} is a deliberate role-signal: any `*mark` is a specific-instance handle; the first syllable carries the side — `hall-` what we forged, `touch-` what we captured.
The touchmark is the GAR path segment: the Lode package is `{gar_lodes_namespace}/«TOUCHMARK»`, so one value names the whole package.
See {rbst_touchmark} for the string type and regex.

[[rbtga_lode_vouch]]
//axl_voices axig_verification_artifact
{rbtga_lode_vouch}::
The provenance envelope carried by every
{rbtga_lode}
— one per Lode, batch-level — recording *capture-fidelity* rather than build-integrity.
The fetched-side parallel to the made-side
{rbtga_ark_vouch},
and a different claim: "we faithfully captured upstream X at digest D," not "we built this securely."
+
It has one canonical home: the in-GAR `:rbi_vouch` tag (the Lode self-describing in the registry, decoded by {rbtgo_lode_augur}). The host-side {rbf_fact_lode} carries only the bare touchmark, not the envelope — so reading host-side provenance costs a GAR call, the accepted price of a single canonical home.
+
Fields (schema `rbld-vouch-1`, near-term unsigned — `signature: null` — but schema-versioned):
`schema`, `kind`, `lode` (the touchmark), `acquired_at` (ISO-8601 UTC), `acquired_by` (the capturing Cloud Build service account), `capture_build` (the capture's own build id), `trust_grade`, and `members[]`.
Each `members[]` entry carries `name`, `origin`, `digest`, `verification`, and its assigned `tags` — so the captured identity and any Director-assigned names are themselves attested.
`members[]` is the cardinality axis: length 1 for singletons (bole), N for cohorts.
+
Two honest trust grades, declared per Lode (Palisade honesty — never over-claim):
+
* `verified-against-published` — bytes re-checkable against a persistent upstream (OCI content-address, or an out-of-band published checksum). The bole kind carries this grade.
* `recorded-at-acquisition` — upstream offers no durable re-checkable reference (e.g. quay rotates podvm images out within days), so RB attests only the digest observed at capture. Reserved for the podvm kinds.
+
Future signing is reserved via cosign or the OCI referrers API once GAR maturity is confirmed.

=== Foundry Fact Files

[[rbf_fact_hallmark]]
//axpof_fact
{rbf_fact_hallmark}::
Hallmark string.

[[rbf_fact_build_id]]
//axpof_fact
{rbf_fact_build_id}::
Cloud Build job ID.

[[rbf_fact_gar_root]]
//axpof_fact
{rbf_fact_gar_root}::
GAR registry root path.

[[rbf_fact_ark_stem]]

185	CB pre-populates GAR credentials; no explicit docker login step is needed.
186	See RBSOB superseded notice.
187	
188	==== Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools
189	
190	Egress lockdown is implemented through the dual-pool architecture.
191	The airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`) blocks all public internet access.
192	Operations that require upstream access (inscribe, enshrine) run on the tether pool.
193	Operations that must be isolated (about, vouch) always run on the airgap pool.
194	Conjure and bind are routed per vessel's `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
195	
196	**Dependency mirroring is universal.**
197	All GCB step images come from reliquaries (inscribed tool images in GAR).
198	All base images come from anchors (enshrined base images in GAR).
199	This is not just an airgap feature — it is a universal robustness measure against upstream registry churn.
200	
201	**Operation pool routing:**
202	
203	[cols="1,1,2",options="header"]
204	|===
205	|Operation
206	|Pool
207	|Rationale
208	
209	|Inscribe

file.content:
CB pre-populates GAR credentials; no explicit docker login step is needed.
See RBSOB superseded notice.

==== Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools

Egress lockdown is implemented through the dual-pool architecture.
The airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`) blocks all public internet access.
Operations that require upstream access (inscribe, enshrine) run on the tether pool.
Operations that must be isolated (about, vouch) always run on the airgap pool.
Conjure and bind are routed per vessel's `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.

**Dependency mirroring is universal.**
All GCB step images come from reliquaries (inscribed tool images in GAR).
All base images come from anchors (enshrined base images in GAR).
This is not just an airgap feature — it is a universal robustness measure against upstream registry churn.

**Operation pool routing:**

content.text:
Oriented. Here's what's there and what isn't.

## README: no, there's no such section

The README never mentions **Lodes**, **Quay**, or **registry churn** at all. Closest neighbors:

- The **Eventual Consistency** appendix (`README.md:506`) is the complaint you're thinking of — but it's strictly about GCP's *missing completion contract*, nothing about upstream image volatility.
- **Build Isolation** appendix already gestures at the *mechanism* without naming the grievance: Reliquary tool images "inscribed once and pinned by digest" (`:495`) and base images "digest-pinned at Enshrine time… stored as project-owned copies in the Depot's registry" (`:500`). That's anchoring-against-churn described as a feature, never as a reaction to a vendor's bad behavior.
- The **Roadmap** has a "Podman Support" entry (`:566`) but it's about runtime choice on macOS — unrelated to the podvm *image* churn problem.

So the README has the rationale-as-complaint pattern for Google, and the anchoring *machinery* described neutrally, but no parallel complaint for upstream registry churn. Your instinct is right — there's a gap.

## RBS0 and siblings: yes, it's treated — but as terse mechanics, not a complaint

The phenomenon you hit is named almost verbatim in the spec:

- **RBS0:2218** — the `recorded-at-acquisition` trust grade: *"upstream offers no durable re-checkable reference (e.g. quay rotates podvm images out within days), so RB attests only the digest observed at capture. Reserved for the podvm kinds."* This is the canonical citation — Quay churn is the *reason that whole trust grade exists*.
- **RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture:199** — universal mirroring is *"not just an airgap feature — it is a universal robustness measure against upstream registry churn."* The general principle.
- **RBSLA-lode_augur:85** — augur reports trust honestly, *"never that it remains re-checkable against a vanished upstream."*
- The entire **Lode** model (RBS0:2150+) is the structural answer: cloud-side capture of an upstream artifact into a project-owned GAR package with a provenance envelope. Lodes *are* the anchor you're describing.

The spec treats it in the Palisade register — honest trust grades, no over-claiming — but it's mechanical and vocabulary-bound. It documents *what RB does about churn*; it doesn't editorialize about Quay's *practice* the way the README editorializes about "eventual consistency."

## The shape of the gap

A README complaint section would be doing two things at once: (1) registering the grievance (Quay/podvm rapid rotation broke you, asynchronously), and (2) introducing Lodes to README readers for the first time — since the README is currently silent on the whole capture side. Natural home is right beside the Eventual Consistency appendix (`:506`), as a sibling "why we capture upstream" / "the disappearing-upstream problem" appendix; the spec evidence above gives you the load-bearing facts to draw from.

Want me to draft it?

## Paces

### lode-podvm-cerebro-experiment (₢BHAAK) [rough]

## Character
Hands-on registry-tool experiment on the cerebro Linux host requiring judgment, then spec authoring and a defer-or-implement decision. The deliverable is evidence + a recorded decision, NOT working cloud code. Mount cold: this docket carries the whole 260608 design chat because that chat is gone.

## Goal
Establish — by experiment on cerebro, not by inheriting the GHCR prototype — how Recipe Bottle brings podman-VM machine-os images under its control in GAR. Temp-install crane/skopeo/oras on cerebro, capture BOTH quay families into the depot `rbi_ld` GAR with provenance, characterize the live artifact shape and which tool handles it cleanly, then record findings into the specs and write the cloud-layer implementation decision. The production cloud capture (a future `rbgjl05` step + `rbldv_` body + theurge fixture, riding the spine like underpin/conclave) is a SEPARATE follow-up pace this experiment unblocks — do NOT build it here.

## Cinched
- **Venue: cerebro, by express exception.** The workstation is `bash`/`curl`/`openssl`/`jq` + coreutils ONLY (README.md opening — the tiny-dependency surface is a stated product value); it cannot and must not host image tools. cerebro (`ssh cerebro`, intel-Linux, Claude Code installed) gets express, exceptional permission to download image-manipulation apps (crane, skopeo, oras). They are throwaway — removal is a Done-when gate.
- **skopeo is ruled OUT as the podvm capture tool** — wrong failure profile for a vendor-churned format: it hard-rejects benign OCI/Docker media-type mixing (go-containerregistry issue 1608) and SILENTLY skips foreign/non-distributable layers by default (skopeo issue 545) → hollow-mirror risk under the recorded grade. The real contest is **crane vs oras**; prior lean is **crane** (Google's own go-containerregistry tool; already proven on our GCB/GAR in the retired RBSOB OCI-layout bridge; `crane blob` is get-by-digest-or-error, the loud failure the recorded grade wants; robust across podman's image→OCI-artifact migration). The experiment confirms or overturns this — it does not assume it.
- **Cloud-side acquisition premise STANDS.** cerebro is the lab bench for THIS experiment only; production capture remains cloud-side (paddock "acquisition runs cloud-side, never the workstation" is untouched). cerebro is never the production capture path. If the experiment tempts a cerebro-hosted production capture, that is a premise-level reversal — escalate, don't drift.
- **Both quay families**, captured + characterized: `quay.io/podman/machine-os` (native, kind-letter `vn`) and `quay.io/podman/machine-os-wsl` (wsl, kind-letter `vw`). They diverge structurally; one does not characterize the other.
- **Auth: copy the Director RBRA to cerebro** for the GAR push (simplest; the identity host-side `divine`/`banish` already use against `rbi_ld`). The copied RBRA is sensitive and temporary — removal is part of teardown. Operator owns the post-pace credential hygiene (payor reauthorization + governor remantle) as a consequence of exposing the Director RBRA on cerebro — NOT a pace step.
- **Curated platform subset per family, never a full mirror** (paddock podvm-selective premise — quay's index is 5-15 GB). **recorded-at-acquisition** trust grade (quay publishes no durable checksum; rotates within days).
- **Consumption is NOT attempted** — no `podman machine init`/boot of the captured image. Capture + control-in-GAR only.
- **Captured Lodes are banished at teardown** (clean experiment; proves the full capture → divine → banish lifecycle).

## Method (experiment recipe — adapt at mount, not a rigid script)
- On cerebro: install crane + skopeo + oras as throwaway static binaries into a scratch dir (cleanest teardown — crane/oras ship static Go binaries); copy the Director RBRA; confirm egress to `quay.io` (pull) and GAR (push).
- For EACH family at a chosen podman version: inspect the upstream manifest with each of the three tools. Record the durable characterization — is it an image-manifest or an OCI artifact-manifest (`artifactType`)? what media types do the disk blobs carry? any foreign/non-distributable markers? which tool reads/extracts it cleanly, which errors? This characterization is the core finding the cloud decision rests on.
- Capture a curated platform subset of each family into `rbi_ld/<vn|vw stamp>` in the depot GAR, with a provenance envelope mirroring the wsl/reliquary `:rbi_vouch` shape (members[] is the cardinality axis).
- Prove control from the WORKSTATION: the existing `rbw-ld` (divine) enumerates the captured Lodes and `rbw-lB` (banish) deletes them — these are curl/REST host ops, tool-agnostic about how the Lode was made, so a cerebro-captured Lode is workstation-controllable today.
- Verify the anti-hollow-mirror guard: confirm each disk blob is actually present/sized in GAR via REST read, not merely a manifest. (When this goes cloud-side later, this guard must be GAR-REST/curl host-side, never a host tool.)

## Done when
- Both families captured into the depot `rbi_ld` GAR from cerebro with provenance, and shown controllable via the existing host-side `divine` + `banish`.
- The per-family characterization (manifest shape, disk-blob media types, foreign markers, crane-vs-oras-vs-skopeo verdict) is recorded into RBSPV (`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc` — consider promoting out of FUTURE/ as podvm activates) and the relevant RBS* Lode subdocuments; a memo is written only if findings warrant durable capture beyond the spec edits.
- The cloud-layer implementation decision is written down plainly: which tool, what the future `rbgjl05` must do, what `rbldv_` body + theurge fixture the follow-up pace builds — enough that a cold mount executes it without re-deriving.
- Teardown verified: image tools removed from cerebro (`which crane skopeo oras` → nothing), copied Director RBRA removed, captured experimental Lodes banished. GAR and cerebro left as found.

## Sources (re-derive nothing)
- Prototype: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` + RBSPV — crane manifest/blob navigation, two-family split, FROM-scratch repackage, the ignite-VM that cloud-side dissolves.
- Cloud shape to copy in the FOLLOW-UP pace (not here): `rbldw_Underpin.sh` (opaque-blob body), `rbldr_Reliquary.sh` (multi-member cohort body — podvm is opaque-blob × multi-member, a blend of these two), `rblds_Spine.sh` (capture-spine contract, RBSCJ), cloud steps `rbgjl02`/`03`/`04`, shared snippets `rbgjs/`.
- Durable web anchors: skopeo strict media-type (github.com/google/go-containerregistry issues/1608); skopeo silent foreign-layer skip (github.com/containers/skopeo issues/545); podman→OCI-artifact direction (podman 5.0 `--artifact`, 5.4 preview `podman artifact`, 5.5 `artifact extract`; containers/podman issues/24785 disk-artifact manifest type); oras artifact-native (oras.land).
- Registration surface the FOLLOW-UP cloud pace touches (kind-letters already reserved `RBGC_LODE_KIND_PODVM_NATIVE="vn"` / `_PODVM_WSL="vw"`): `rbgc_Constants.sh` brands/tags, `rbldl_Lifecycle.sh` divine legend, and the theurge fixture registry across `rbtdrc_crucible.rs` + `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — including the single-fixture `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` lookup registry that the 12f48cbab spook proved is easy to miss.
- Workstation restriction: README.md opening. cerebro access: CLAUDE.md "Test Environments". Paddock premises: cloud-side acquisition, podvm-selective retention, recorded grade, Palisade honesty.

### onboarding-clean-tree-gate (₢BHAAR) [rough]

## Character
Cross-track onboarding edit — one operator-facing convention across tracks, not a one-track flip. Mechanical once scope confirms; scope rides two upstream design calls.

## Goal
Teach the uniform "tools never commit, gate on a clean tree" convention in onboarding. Today "commit first" is taught only in the kludge track (rbhoct) because kludge is the sole gating verb; once the fact-chaining pace makes the gate uniform, the Director install-gesture tracks newly refuse on a dirty tree with no prose warning.

## Locked
- Operator-facing half only — the clean-tree gate. The PREV/fact-chaining mechanism is dispatch-internal; do NOT teach it in onboarding.
- Crash Course (rbhocc, already owns "diagnostic failure") is the concept home; surface the commit-between-steps prompt at the Director tracks (first-build/airgap/bind/graft).

## Scope contingencies — both upstream, both landed by mount; read them before writing prose
- Gate placement: this pace assumes a pre-condition gate (refuse-before-write, the kludge model). If the fact-chaining pace placed the gate downstream-only (install leaves the tree dirty, only charge refuses — the rbob_charge model), the affected surface shifts.
- Election shape: if the bole cutover made election a SEPARATE operator step, the cutover tracks owe the operator a new step (not just a vocab rename) and this pace's scope widens. If election is a cycle (one command, kludge-style), rename-flip + this gate teaching suffices.

## Discovery recipe
grep the handbook for existing commit/clean-tree prose (rbhoct is the known site); enumerate the Director install-gesture tracks under rbho* that newly gate.

## Done
The convention is taught once as a Crash Course concept and surfaced at every onboarding track that newly gates; a new operator following the Director tracks is warned to commit before the next gated step rather than hitting an unexplained refusal.

### lode-vocab-finalization-scrub (₢BHAAD) [rough]

## Character
Terminal heat-wide pass — mechanical but exacting. The single danger is a kill-stem that also lives inside a word we keep; the KEEP guards below are load-bearing, not courtesy. Runs LAST, after every cutover. Never before.

## Goal
Once all cutovers have repointed their chokepoints and banished their old GAR namespaces, sweep the whole repo so NO vocabulary superseded by this heat survives — in code, specs, tabtargets, onboarding prose, comments, or constant names — while leaving the introduced Lode vocabulary and the legitimately-overloaded survivors untouched.

## Why this pace is terminal (do not front-run)
This is the delete-old-last tail of the heat. Each cutover deletes its own namespace in-place; until a cutover lands, its surviving old vocabulary IS the rollback. This pace catches only what the per-cutover deletes could not reach — stray prose, comments, cross-refs, a mis-filed spec, a vestigial constant name. It MUST remain the final pace; nothing scrubs shared vocabulary before that vocabulary's cutover has landed.

## KILL — these stems must not survive (except where a KEEP guard applies)
- `enshrine`, `enshrinement`, `Enshrines`, `*Enshrinements` (tabtarget frontispieces) — superseded by `ensconce` / `bole`
- `rbi_es` — enshrinement GAR namespace, folded into `rbi_ld`
- `rbi_rq` — reliquary GAR namespace, folded into `rbi_ld`
- `reliquary stamp`, and `reliquary` used as a standalone identifier-noun — replaced by the reliquary-kind **touchmark**
- `rbfl_inscribe` and the reliquary-mirror sense of `inscribe` — absorbed into `fetter` / `conclave`
- `RBSAE-ark_enshrine` (whole file) and its `rbtgo_ark_enshrine` quoin — superseded by `RBSLE` / `rbtgo_lode_ensconce`. (RBSAE is mis-filed under `ark_` though it is fetched-side base capture; delete it, do not adapt it.)
- constant names carrying `ENSHRINE` (e.g. `ZRBFC_BUILD_POLL_CEILING_ENSHRINE`) — verb-neutralize the name
- `rbf_fact_lode` quoin and the `<stamp>.lode` multi-form capture-file — retired in favor of single-form chaining facts (a touchmark value-fact + a kind-brand enum); catch any stragglers the fact-chaining pace's RBSLE reslate left in place.

## KEEP — never flag these (introduced or legitimately retained)
- New Lode vocab: `lode`, `rbi_ld`, `rbld*` (the family: rbld0_/rblds_/rbldb_/rbldl_), `RBSL*`, `bole`, `ensconce`, `divine`, `banish`, `touchmark`, the inspect verb `augur` (`rbw-la`, now minted), and the introduced RBS0 quoins (`rbtga_lode`, `rbtga_touchmark`, `rbtga_lode_vouch`, `gar_lodes_namespace`, `rbst_touchmark`, `rbtgog_lode`, `rbtgo_lode_*`)
- `reliquary` as a **kind name** survives (it is `conclave`'s kind) — only the standalone noun / "stamp" identifier dies
- `rbrd_inscribe` (depot tripwire, `rbw-rdi`) — different operation, OUT of scope, stays
- `rbfd_mirror` (bind vessel-mode copy, made-side) — OUT of scope, stays

## Discovery recipe
`grep -rinE 'enshrine|rbi_es|rbi_rq|reliquary stamp|rbfl_inscribe|rbf_fact_lode|\.lode\b' Tools/ tt/` → expect zero. For bare `inscribe` and bare `reliquary` (both overloaded), grep and hand-classify every hit against the KEEP guards above. This list ACCRETES: as each cutover lands, append any stragglers it could not delete in place.

## Done
The recipe returns zero un-guarded hits; every KEEP term is intact; `fast` plus the relevant suites stay green. The heat's superseded vocabulary is gone from the repo.

### theurge-fixture-fact-chain-fix (₢BHAAS) [rough]

## Character
Test-infra fix. The cutover's verify gate is structurally blind — make it see. Root cause is known and proven live; the mechanism is mount's call.

## Goal
Make the airgap-chain fixture genuinely exercise the bole derived-pull election, so the cutover gains a real automated regression guard. Today it false-greens.

## Problem
`zrbfd_elect_base_anchor` reads the bole touchmark from the depth-1 BURD `previous/` chain (ensconce emits it; the next ordain dispatch reads it). theurge isolates every tabtarget invocation in its own `burv_output` root (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`), so `bud_dispatch`'s `current/`->`previous/` promotion never chains across invokes — ordain's `BURD_PREVIOUS_DIR` is empty, the election takes its no-op guard, and the conjure builds from the lingering `rbi_es` base: green but meaningless. The real operator flow chains because sequential tabtargets share `../output-buk`.

## Done
The fixture drives ensconce->ordain so the touchmark reaches the election, then asserts the forge anchor became `rbi_ld/<touchmark>:rbi_bole` and the conjure built from it. A non-firing election fails the fixture.

## Cinched
- Restore depth-1 chaining only for invokes meant to chain; don't collapse `burv` isolation for the rest.
- The election itself is correct — proven live this heat: back-to-back ensconce->ordain on a forge vessel repointed the anchor `rbi_es`->`rbi_ld` and the conjure built+vouched green from it. The defect is in harness invoke-isolation, not the election.
